plain_language: Alameda County's mitigation page identifies wildfires, floods, and earthquakes as common natural disasters the Bay Area may face, and frames preparedness as a way to reduce risk before disaster and shorten recovery afterward.
recommended_action: Use this as countywide fallback: secure tall furniture and heavy items, practice Drop, Cover, and Hold On, and keep supplies for several days without normal utilities.
recovery_question: If your home cannot be occupied after shaking, where would your household stay and how would you access documents, medications, and transportation?

plain_language: Alameda County's mitigation page treats flood as one of the common natural disaster risks in the county. Address-level flood status should still come from the FEMA layer check when available.
recommended_action: Keep critical documents above floor level, know a route to higher ground, and avoid walking or driving through floodwater.
recovery_question: If water enters your home, how will you document damage, contact insurance or your landlord, and find temporary housing during cleanup?

plain_language: Alameda County's mitigation page identifies wildfire as a common natural disaster risk. This is countywide planning context and does not mean the address has been checked inside a fire hazard zone.
recommended_action: Sign up for official alerts, prepare a go-bag, identify evacuation routes, and plan for smoke, pets, medications, and transportation.
recovery_question: If evacuation or smoke keeps you away from home, where would you stay and how would you keep work, school, pets, medications, and finances stable?

plain_language: Oakland's LHMP treats earthquake as a major citywide hazard involving shaking, liquefaction, vulnerable buildings, lifeline disruption, displacement, and shelter needs.
recommended_action: Treat earthquake preparedness as a whole-household priority even if the app has not completed an address-level seismic layer check.
recovery_question: If shaking damages your building or utilities, where will your household stay and how will you reconnect with family, work, school, and medical support?

plain_language: Oakland's LHMP discusses flood exposure in low-lying and shoreline areas. StayReady should pair this city context with the address-point FEMA flood check when available.
recommended_action: If flood is shown, review the address-level result first, then prepare documents, insurance contacts, and a higher-ground route.
recovery_question: If your home has water damage, how will you document it before cleanup and avoid unsafe mold, electrical, or contaminated-water conditions?

plain_language: Oakland's LHMP identifies wildfire as a serious local hazard, especially where hills, vegetation, smoke, evacuation constraints, and vulnerable communities intersect.
recommended_action: Prepare for early evacuation: alerts, go-bag, medications, pets, vehicle or transit backup, and two routes out if possible.
recovery_question: If fire, smoke, road closures, or utility loss make home unsafe, where will your household stay and how will you cover immediate costs?

plain_language: Berkeley's 2024 LHMP ranks earthquake as likely and catastrophic. The Hayward Fault crosses eastern Berkeley, the surface rupture zone runs along the base of the Berkeley hills, and mapped liquefaction zones include the Berkeley Marina/Eastshore State Park, Codornices Creek, Strawberry Creek, northwest Berkeley from the shoreline to San Pablo Avenue, and southwest Berkeley from the shoreline to McGee Avenue.
recommended_action: Treat earthquake preparedness as citywide in Berkeley: secure heavy items, practice Drop, Cover, and Hold On, prepare for post-earthquake fire and utility disruption, and use address/fault/liquefaction checks where available.
recovery_question: If shaking, liquefaction, fire following earthquake, or utility failure makes your Berkeley home unsafe, where will you stay and how will you reach documents, medications, transportation, school, work, and caregivers?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Berkeley 2024 LHMP PDF: hazard ranking and Earthquake section, pages B-20 to B-56.

plain_language: Berkeley's LHMP flood materials identify creek flooding, storm drain overflow, Berkeley floodplains, and storm drain infrastructure limits. The public slide appendix says some storm drain components are over 90 years old and designed around 2 inches of rain in 6 hours.
recommended_action: Use the address flood-layer result first, then protect your home by directing water away from the building, watching where water accumulates, considering flood insurance, learning whether sandbags are useful for your property, and avoiding walking, swimming, or driving through floodwater.
recovery_question: If your Berkeley home floods, how will you document losses, handle cleanup safely, contact insurance or your landlord, and manage power/internet outage, temporary housing, medications, school, and work?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Berkeley 2024 LHMP PDF flood appendix slides, pages 556 to 580.

plain_language: Berkeley's 2024 LHMP ranks wildland-urban-interface fire as likely and catastrophic. The plan identifies Fire Zones 2 and 3 in the Berkeley hills as high concern, Panoramic Hill as the greatest WUI vulnerability, and notes that wind-driven fire under Extreme Fire Weather can move from the hills into the flatlands.
recommended_action: If you are in or near the Berkeley hills, prepare to leave before Extreme Fire Weather when officials recommend it; keep alerts on, plan transportation for narrow/limited evacuation routes, harden defensible space where applicable, and prepare for smoke and power disruption citywide.
recovery_question: If wildfire, smoke, or evacuation affects your Berkeley household, where will you stay and how will you handle medications, pets, children, school/work continuity, transportation, insurance, and short-term costs?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Berkeley 2024 LHMP PDF: WUI Fire section, pages B-56 to B-89.

plain_language: Alameda's CAHMP page says the plan addresses earthquakes and directs residents to prepare homes, retrofit where appropriate, check soft-story apartment listings, secure movable items, reduce chimney risk, prevent gas leaks, and consider earthquake insurance.
recommended_action: Secure furniture, appliances, and water heaters; check soft-story or retrofit needs; know gas shutoff basics; and keep documents, medications, and insurance information reachable.
recovery_question: If shaking damages your building or cuts utilities, where will you stay and how will you access documents, medicines, and transportation?

plain_language: Alameda's CAHMP page says the plan addresses floods, sea level and groundwater rise, coastal erosion, tsunamis, heat, smoky air, drought, and dam failure inundation, and residents should use address-based hazard tools to understand local earthquake, flood, and tsunami hazards.
recommended_action: Use the FEMA address check and MyHazards-style address tools when available, then plan for higher ground, document protection, insurance questions, and temporary housing if water damage occurs.
recovery_question: If floodwater affects your home, what is your plan for documenting damage, safe cleanup, temporary housing, and transportation disruptions?

plain_language: Alameda's CAHMP page specifically includes smoky air as a climate and natural-hazard concern, but it does not support a strong claim that a typical Alameda address is inside a wildfire hazard zone.
recommended_action: Prepare for regional smoke, alerts, medications, indoor air protection, and evacuation communications, but do not treat this as proof that an Alameda address is inside a wildfire hazard zone.
recovery_question: If smoke or regional evacuation impacts your household, how will you protect health, pets, school/work continuity, and temporary housing needs?

plain_language: Hayward's LRP includes a mapped risk assessment for earthquake, including Hayward Fault scenario shaking, liquefaction hazard, tsunami coastal evacuation zones, and earthquake-related landscape hazards.
recommended_action: Prepare for earthquake disruption by securing heavy items, planning for utility outages, checking whether your household relies on elevators or medical power, and keeping transportation and medication backups ready.
recovery_question: If roads, elevators, utilities, or buildings are disrupted after shaking, how will your household reach shelter, caregivers, work, school, and medical support?

plain_language: Hayward's LRP includes flood zones and sea-level-rise inundation mapping. It notes that sea level rise can make existing flood-prone areas flood more often and can reduce stormwater drainage performance, causing street and basement flooding.
recommended_action: Check whether the saved address matched the flood layer, prepare document protection, know safe routes away from flooded streets, and plan for longer-duration flooding near shoreline or drainage-affected areas.
recovery_question: If flooding damages your home or blocks travel, how will you document losses, find housing, and maintain work, school, and medical access?

plain_language: Hayward's LRP maps high fire hazard areas and notes drought can increase wildfire hazard through drier vegetation and reduced water supply for firefighting, especially in wildland-urban-interface areas.
recommended_action: Prepare alerts, evacuation routes, medications, pets, transportation backup, and smoke/power-disruption plans before wildfire or red-flag conditions.
recovery_question: If wildfire or smoke affects home access, how will you handle temporary housing, medications, pets, finances, and school/work continuity?

plain_language: The Tri-City LHMP covers Fremont and identifies earthquake among the natural hazards assessed for local mitigation planning.
recommended_action: Prepare for shaking, utility disruption, transportation impacts, and family reunification; use this as jurisdiction context, not parcel-level risk.
recovery_question: If your Fremont home is unsafe after shaking, where will your household stay and how will you reach work, school, caregivers, and medical needs?

plain_language: The Tri-City LHMP covers Fremont and includes flooding in the assessed natural hazards. Address-level flood status should still come from the FEMA layer check.
recommended_action: Review the flood-layer result, avoid flooded roads, protect documents, and plan for housing or transit disruption after water damage.
recovery_question: If flooding affects your home or commute, how will you document damage, contact insurance or your landlord, and keep medications and work access stable?

plain_language: The Tri-City LHMP covers Fremont and includes wildland/urban-interface fires in its hazard planning scope.
recommended_action: Prepare alerts, evacuation routes, smoke protection, medications, pets, and backup transportation before fire-weather conditions.
recovery_question: If fire, smoke, or road closures disrupt your household, how will you handle temporary housing, pets, finances, and school/work continuity?

plain_language: Newark is covered by the Tri-City LHMP, which includes earthquake among natural hazards assessed for the planning area.
recommended_action: Prepare for shaking, utility disruption, transportation delays, and family reunification; avoid treating this as address-level risk.
recovery_question: If your Newark residence is unsafe, where will you stay and how will you keep documents, medications, work, and school needs reachable?

plain_language: Newark is covered by the Tri-City LHMP, which includes flooding among assessed natural hazards. Use FEMA address results for point-level flood evidence.
recommended_action: Protect documents, know safe routes around flooded streets, and plan how to document damage before cleanup.
recovery_question: If flood damage affects your home, how will you handle insurance, landlord contact, temporary housing, transportation, and safe cleanup?

plain_language: Newark is covered by the Tri-City LHMP, which includes wildland/urban-interface fire in its regional hazard planning scope.
recommended_action: Use wildfire guidance for alerts, smoke, evacuation readiness, pets, medications, and backup transportation unless stronger address-level fire-zone evidence is added.
recovery_question: If smoke or evacuation affects your household, what is the plan for health needs, pet care, work/school continuity, housing, and urgent costs?

plain_language: Union City is covered by the Tri-City LHMP, which includes earthquake among natural hazards assessed for local mitigation planning.
recommended_action: Secure heavy items, practice Drop, Cover, and Hold On, and plan for utilities, transportation, family reunification, and medical continuity.
recovery_question: If shaking disrupts home access, utilities, or transportation, where will your household stay and how will you continue school, work, and medical routines?

plain_language: Union City is covered by the Tri-City LHMP, which includes flooding among assessed hazards. FEMA address checks remain the strongest point-level flood evidence.
recommended_action: Review the flood-layer result, avoid floodwater, protect documents, and plan for transportation and temporary housing disruption.
recovery_question: If flood damage occurs, how will you document losses, arrange safe cleanup, contact insurance or landlord support, and cover short-term costs?

plain_language: Union City is covered by the Tri-City LHMP, which includes wildland/urban-interface fire in the regional hazard planning scope.
recommended_action: Prepare for alerts, smoke, evacuation, medications, pets, transportation, and finances before fire-weather or smoke events.
recovery_question: If wildfire smoke or evacuation disrupts your home, how will your household handle temporary housing, health needs, pets, and school/work continuity?

plain_language: The Tri-Valley Volume 2 annex ranks earthquake as Dublin's second highest hazard with a high risk score, based on the Haywired Hayward Fault scenario. The annex also notes portions of Downtown Dublin lie within the Alquist-Priolo Earthquake Fault Zone.
recommended_action: For a Dublin address, use the app's mapped-fault proximity check where available, then prepare for shaking, utility disruption, temporary shelter, medication access, school/work continuity, and communications through AC Alert/EverBridge.
recovery_question: If shaking damages your Dublin home, workplace, utilities, or route out of Downtown Dublin, where will you stay and how will you maintain documents, medications, transportation, school, work, and finances?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Dublin annex pages 1-3 to 1-13.

plain_language: The Dublin annex ranks flood lower than earthquake and wildfire, but it identifies FEMA flood-zone context in parts of Downtown Dublin and undeveloped Eastern Dublin parcels along I-580. It also notes a February 1999 flash-flood/severe winter weather event that damaged public facilities and says citywide storm-drain conditions need assessment.
recommended_action: Use the address-level FEMA flood check first; if flood is shown, protect documents, know safe routes around I-580/Downtown/Eastern Dublin drainage issues, avoid floodwater, and review insurance or landlord responsibilities.
recovery_question: If stormwater or mapped floodplain flooding affects your Dublin home or commute, how will you document damage, arrange cleanup, find temporary housing, and keep work, school, medications, and transportation stable?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Dublin annex pages 1-3 to 1-13.

plain_language: The Dublin annex ranks wildfire as a medium hazard and documents two 2017 wildfire incidents: a 50-acre fire that triggered automated alerts to about 150 residents and evacuation to a city-sponsored shelter, and a 75-acre Camp Parks fire with road closures and a city-sponsored reunification center.
recommended_action: Use the address fire-zone check first, then prepare Dublin wildfire readiness around AC Alert/EverBridge, road closures, shelter/reunification locations, pets, medications, transportation, and smoke impacts.
recovery_question: If wildfire, smoke, or road closures force a Dublin evacuation, where will you reunify and stay, and how will you keep pets, medications, school/work continuity, documents, and urgent costs covered?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Dublin annex pages 1-8 to 1-13.

plain_language: The Livermore annex ranks earthquake as the city's highest hazard. It records moderate structural damage from the 1980 Greenville Fault earthquake and identifies earthquake vulnerability concerns for road undercrossings and creek bridges/culverts, including Isabel, Murrieta, P Street, Livermore Avenue, Arroyo Mocho, Arroyo Las Positas, Vasco Road, Central Avenue, and Stanley Boulevard facilities.
recommended_action: For Livermore, treat earthquake recovery as a transportation-and-utilities problem as well as a home-safety problem: secure heavy items, prepare for road/bridge disruption, plan backup routes, and keep documents, medications, power, and contacts accessible.
recovery_question: If shaking closes an undercrossing, damages a creek crossing, or interrupts utilities, how will your household reach shelter, caregivers, work, school, medicine, and financial support?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Livermore annex pages 2-3 to 2-13.

plain_language: The Livermore annex ranks flood lower than earthquake and wildfire, but it records past severe winter storm and flash-flood events and says FEMA maps do not adequately address storm-drain inundation. It identifies storm-drain pump backup needs at Isabel Avenue, Murrieta Boulevard, P Street, L Street, and Livermore Avenue road/rail undercrossings.
recommended_action: Use the address FEMA flood check first, but also plan for storm-drain and undercrossing flooding: avoid flooded roads, protect documents, know alternate routes, and prepare for cleanup, insurance, and temporary housing needs.
recovery_question: If flooding blocks an undercrossing or damages your Livermore home, how will you document losses, arrange repairs, maintain transportation, and keep school, work, medication, and finances stable?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Livermore annex pages 2-7 to 2-13.

plain_language: The Livermore annex ranks wildfire as a medium hazard. It says moderate-to-high wildfire severity areas are primarily on city edges where urban development abuts agricultural and open-space areas, and that there are no very-high wildfire severity areas within city limits.
recommended_action: Use the address fire-zone check first, then prepare for edge-of-city wildfire, smoke, road access, pets, medications, backup transportation, and household evacuation needs.
recovery_question: If wildfire, smoke, or access-road disruption affects your Livermore household, where will you stay and how will you keep pets, health needs, work/school, transportation, and finances stable?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Livermore annex pages 2-3 to 2-13.

plain_language: The Pleasanton annex ranks earthquake as a high hazard, second after wildfire, based on the Haywired Hayward Fault scenario. The annex notes Pleasanton's west side includes the seismically active Pleasanton and Main Ridges and that Downtown Pleasanton includes older buildings from the late 1890s.
recommended_action: For a Pleasanton address, combine the app's fault-proximity check with household preparedness: secure heavy items, plan for older-building or utility disruption, identify backup transportation, and keep documents, medications, pets, school, and work needs reachable.
recovery_question: If shaking damages your Pleasanton home, older downtown building, utilities, or commute route, where will you stay and how will you continue medications, pets, transportation, school, work, and finances?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Pleasanton annex pages 3-1 to 3-11.

plain_language: The Pleasanton annex ranks flood as low compared with wildfire and earthquake, but it still records severe winter storms/flooding in 1996, identifies areas likely to experience flooding and climate-change effects, and shows the city participates in CRS Class 8 with 139 flood-insurance policies and 22 total loss claims reported as of June 2017.
recommended_action: Use the address FEMA flood result first; if flood is relevant, avoid floodwater, protect documents, review insurance or landlord responsibilities, and plan for travel disruption around local streams, roads, and drainage systems.
recovery_question: If flooding affects your Pleasanton home or route, how will you document damage, contact insurance or landlord support, arrange cleanup, and cover urgent housing, transport, medication, school, and work needs?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Pleasanton annex pages 3-7 to 3-11.

plain_language: The Pleasanton annex ranks wildfire as Pleasanton's highest hazard, based on Very High and High Fire Severity Zones. It identifies the Southeast Hills and the steep Pleasanton/Main Ridges as part of the local terrain context and carries forward annual defensible-space/public-education work with Livermore-Pleasanton Fire.
recommended_action: Use the address fire-zone check first; if the address is near hillside or fire-severity areas, prepare alerts, evacuation routes, defensible space, medications, pets, backup transportation, smoke protection, and temporary housing options.
recovery_question: If wildfire, smoke, or evacuation affects your Pleasanton household, where will you stay and how will you handle pets, medications, insurance, transportation, school/work continuity, and short-term costs?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Tri-Valley LHMP Volume 2 PDF: Pleasanton annex pages 3-1 to 3-13.

plain_language: Albany Fire Department says the city's LHMP is intended to identify hazards, assess vulnerabilities, and reduce long-term risk, including earthquakes, wildfires, flooding, and other emergencies.
recommended_action: Use Albany guidance as city-level planning context: sign up for AC Alert, make a household plan, secure heavy items, and prepare for utility and transportation disruption.
recovery_question: If an earthquake damages your home or utilities, where will your household stay and how will you access documents, medications, transportation, and school/work needs?

plain_language: Albany Fire Department identifies flooding as one of the emergencies addressed by the city's LHMP and encourages residents to use AC Alert for emergency information.
recommended_action: Use the address-level FEMA flood check when available, keep documents above floor level, know safe routes, and follow AC Alert or city instructions during storms.
recovery_question: If flooding affects your home, how will you document damage, contact insurance or your landlord, arrange cleanup, and manage temporary housing?

plain_language: Albany Fire Department identifies wildfires among hazards addressed by the LHMP and links residents to preparedness, wildfire smoke, and evacuation-related resources.
recommended_action: Sign up for AC Alert, prepare for wildfire smoke, keep medications and documents ready, and review evacuation or shelter plans before fire-weather conditions.
recovery_question: If smoke, evacuation, or power disruption affects your household, how will you protect health, pets, transportation, finances, and school/work continuity?

plain_language: Emeryville's 2025-2030 LHMP identifies earthquake as the hazard with the highest combined likelihood to cause extensive damage. It says Emeryville is about 3 miles from the Hayward Fault, 15 miles from the San Andreas Fault, and 19 miles from the Calaveras Fault; the entire city is in the highest two shaking-potential categories and lies within a liquefaction hazard zone.
recommended_action: Prepare for citywide shaking and liquefaction effects: secure heavy items, plan for water/power/transportation disruption, know whether your building is older or retrofit-sensitive, and keep documents, medications, and backup communications ready.
recovery_question: If liquefaction, utility failure, or building damage disrupts your Emeryville home, where will you stay and how will you access medications, documents, work, school, transportation, and insurance support?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Emeryville 2025-2030 LHMP PDF: Earthquake section, pages 18 to 43.

plain_language: Emeryville's LHMP says most of the city is FEMA Zone X, but local flooding is more a function of the storm drain system than mapped riverine floodplain alone. It also identifies shoreline/marina exposure and notes Lake Temescal Dam failure could flood much of southern Emeryville and reach west and north toward Powell Street within about 25 minutes.
recommended_action: Use the address flood-layer result first, but also prepare for storm-drain flooding, high-tide/storm combinations, dam-failure alerts, and commute disruption around Powell Street, I-80/580, the railroad, shoreline, and marina areas.
recovery_question: If flooding, dam inundation, or storm-drain backup affects your Emeryville home or commute, how will you document damage, find temporary housing, contact insurance or landlord support, and maintain work, school, medication, and transportation needs?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Emeryville 2025-2030 LHMP PDF: Flood/Tsunami/Dam Failure sections, pages 69 to 78.

plain_language: Emeryville's LHMP says the city does not have the terrain or vegetation conditions for large or devastating wildfires. The local concern is more urban fire/conflagration, a brush/vegetation area at Shoreline Park near I-80/580 and the MacArthur Maze, and regional wildfire smoke that can severely affect air quality.
recommended_action: Do not treat Emeryville wildfire as a hills-style WUI claim unless the address fire layer says so; still prepare for smoke, power disruption, urban fire, evacuation-route signs, medications, pets, and transportation disruption.
recovery_question: If smoke, an urban fire, regional wildfire disruption, or road closures affect Emeryville, how will your household protect health, handle pets, keep medications available, and cover temporary housing or commute disruption?
source_detail: Extracted from uploaded Emeryville 2025-2030 LHMP PDF: Wildfires and urban fire discussion, pages 79 to 83.

plain_language: Piedmont's official draft LHMP page says the update studies how Piedmont could be impacted by natural disasters like earthquakes, wildfires, and severe weather and creates a roadmap to reduce or prevent impacts.
recommended_action: Prepare for earthquake damage with secured furniture, emergency supplies, utility shutoff knowledge, and family reunification planning.
recovery_question: If earthquake damage makes your home unsafe, where will your household stay and how will you manage documents, medications, transportation, and school/work continuity?

plain_language: Piedmont's draft LHMP page identifies wildfire as an example of a natural disaster the city is studying for local impact and mitigation actions.
recommended_action: Prepare for alerts, evacuation, smoke, pets, medications, transportation, and defensible-space needs before fire-weather conditions.
recovery_question: If wildfire smoke or evacuation affects your household, how will you handle temporary housing, pets, health needs, finances, and school/work continuity?

plain_language: Piedmont LHMP update materials indicate the city does not have mapped 1% or 0.2% annual chance floodplains, but also note flood hazards are not limited to FEMA mapped floodplains.
recommended_action: Use the FEMA address flood check first; if no mapped layer is found, still prepare for storm drainage, localized flooding, and road access issues during heavy rain.
recovery_question: If heavy rain or localized flooding affects your home or route, how will you document damage, avoid unsafe travel, and maintain medications, school, work, and transportation?

plain_language: San Leandro's Environmental Hazards Element is the city's foundational hazard policy document and addresses hazards including earthquakes, landslides, floods, sea level rise, wildfire, and climate change.
recommended_action: Use this as hazard-element context: secure heavy items, prepare for utility and transportation disruption, and keep documents, medications, and household contacts accessible.
recovery_question: If earthquake damage disrupts your home or transportation, where will your household stay and how will you continue medical, school, work, and financial needs?

plain_language: San Leandro's hazard element context includes floods and sea level rise, and the city update includes climate risk and evacuation planning requirements.
recommended_action: Use FEMA address flood results when available, protect documents, know evacuation or shelter options, and prepare for shoreline, drainage, or transportation disruption.
recovery_question: If flooding affects your home, how will you document damage, reach insurance or landlord support, and manage temporary housing and transportation?

plain_language: San Leandro's hazard element update specifically references wildfire, Very High Fire Hazard Severity Zone review requirements, climate adaptation, and evacuation route analysis.
recommended_action: Prepare for alerts, evacuation routes, smoke, medications, pets, and backup transportation before wildfire or smoke events.
recovery_question: If wildfire smoke, evacuation, or power disruption affects your household, how will you protect health, pets, finances, transportation, and school/work continuity?

plain_language: Alameda County's mitigation planning page provides countywide context for natural hazards, including earthquakes, but community-specific unincorporated-area extraction still needs review.
recommended_action: Use this as county fallback: secure heavy furniture, prepare supplies, sign up for alerts, and plan for utility, transportation, and shelter disruption.
recovery_question: If earthquake damage affects your home or local roads, where will you stay and how will you access documents, medications, caregivers, work, and school?

plain_language: Alameda County's mitigation planning page provides countywide flood context, but unincorporated community-specific flood claims should use address-level FEMA checks or reviewed local chunks.
recommended_action: Use the FEMA address flood check, protect documents, avoid floodwater, and plan for road, utility, and temporary housing disruption.
recovery_question: If flooding affects your home or access roads, how will you document losses, contact insurance or landlord support, and arrange safe cleanup or housing?

plain_language: Alameda County's mitigation planning page provides countywide wildfire context. Unincorporated-area wildfire precision still depends on reviewed local chunks or official fire hazard layers.
recommended_action: Prepare alerts, evacuation routes, go-bags, medications, pets, transportation, and smoke protection before red-flag or regional fire conditions.
recovery_question: If wildfire, smoke, or evacuation disrupts your household, how will you handle temporary housing, health needs, pets, finances, school, and work continuity?
